metadata.system_name: Reference Architecture for Ground-Based Command and Control Systems

evaluation_inputs.system_description: A reference architecture designed to standardize interfaces between software components for various ground-based command and control systems. It serves as a generalized architecture and common infrastructure to facilitate 'plug-and-play' integration, reduce development costs, and support strategic reuse across multiple government and commercial organizations.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The reference architecture was partially documented. The team had developed high-level Interface Design Language (IDL) and completed approximately one-fourth of the IDL for the 12 identified sub-domains. The specification was not complete.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What are the specific quality attribute goals for the system?
How does the architecture support reasoning about tradeoffs between competing attributes like performance and reliability?

hardware_view_description: Ground-based command and control hardware infrastructure
runtime_constraints: Interoperability across autonomous and directed components

questions: How does the system ensure semantic integration between components, not just syntactic connection?
What mechanisms exist to guarantee real-time performance across the distributed middleware?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: A system integrator incorporates a new vendor component into the system.
The system must operate continuously despite a partial network failure.

business_goals: Enable system builders to integrate systems on time and within costs
Drive down the cost of command and control systems
Facilitate 'plug-and-play' integration regardless of component location
technical_constraints: Must use Common Object Request Broker Architecture (CORBA)
Focus on interface definition rather than direct component reuse

architectural_approaches.description: Defines a common infrastructure and standardized Interface Design Language (IDL) for 12 sub-domains to allow independent vendor implementation.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Standardizes the syntax for component interaction.
Decouples component implementation from the interface.

architectural_approaches.description: Uses CORBA as the communication bus for distributed components.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Provides mechanism for diverse objects to communicate.

children.scenario_description: Integrate sub-domains quickly using standard interfaces
Meet real-time performance needs during high-load operations

scenarios.scenario_text: System builders integrate sub-domains quickly using the reference architecture.
Demonstrate 'plug and play' capabilities regardless of component location.

scenario_reference.text: Assessment of the architecture's ability to meet business drivers (performance, availability, modifiability) given the current definition.

architectural_decisions: Delegation of quality attribute satisfaction to integrators
analysis_records.reasoning: The architecture defines syntax but lacks mechanisms for semantic integration or quality attribute management. It explicitly leaves tradeoffs (e.g., cost vs. performance) to the integrator, which contradicts the goal of reducing integration risk.

risks.description: The architecture provides no mechanisms for the integrator to reason about quality attribute tradeoffs.
risks.rationale: Integrators cannot predict system behavior or manage conflicts like real-time performance vs. reliability.

risks.description: There are no overarching quality attribute models (e.g., performance or availability models).
risks.rationale: Without models, there is no way to verify that the composition of components will meet system-level goals.

risks.description: Direct reliance on CORBA poses a risk for evolution.
risks.rationale: Tight coupling to specific middleware limits the ability to change to different technologies in the future.

risks.description: Lack of common support for managing data.
risks.rationale: Absence of a data management strategy may lead to inconsistencies and performance bottlenecks.
